writing-text-files.label: Writing Text Files
writing-text-files.summary: Create or overwrite text files by opening paths in write or append mode and writing text that you can verify with printed output.

sketch0.title: Write mode creates or replaces text

sketch1.title: Append mode adds to the end

sketch2.title: Use newline characters and verify what you wrote

project.title: Practice writing and appending text files

sc-write-vs-append.title: Choose the mode that replaces old text
sc-write-vs-append.prompt: Which file mode should you use when you want to overwrite any existing text in a file with new text?
sc-write-vs-append.hint: Use the question wording and the lesson example to narrow the choices.

help.concept: This question checks a specific idea from the lesson, not general test-taking strategy.
help.hint_1: Compare the prompt with the lesson example and remove choices that do not match the Python behavior.
help.hint_2: Remove options that do not match the concept or evidence named in the question.

sc-verify-written-text.title: Best way to confirm saved text
sc-verify-written-text.prompt: After writing text to a file in this lesson, what is the best way to make the result visible in the output panel?
sc-verify-written-text.hint: Think about reopening the path and showing the saved contents.

help.concept: File-writing exercises should produce observable output, so a common pattern is to reopen the file and print what was written.
help.hint_1: Writing changes the file, but the output panel only shows text you print.
help.hint_2: Choose the option that reads the saved text back and displays it.

options.b: Reopen the file and print its contents

mc-append-true-statements.title: Facts about append mode
mc-append-true-statements.prompt: Choose all true statements about opening a file with append mode (`"a"`).
mc-append-true-statements.hint: Append mode keeps old text and adds new text after it.

help.concept: Append mode writes at the end of existing contents instead of clearing the file first.
help.hint_1: Look for statements that describe keeping existing text.
help.hint_2: Reject any option that says append mode erases the file before writing.

options.b: It adds new text at the end of the file.
options.c: It always removes everything already in the file.
options.d: It can be used to add another line to a log.

mc-good-writing-patterns.title: Useful patterns when writing text files
mc-good-writing-patterns.prompt: Choose all practices that fit this topic's file-writing workflow.
mc-good-writing-patterns.hint: Think about writing text, then making the result observable.

help.concept: Beginner file-writing programs often use `with open(...)` for safe access, newline characters for line breaks, and printed verification after writing.
help.hint_1: Look for patterns involving `with open`, `\n`, and printing saved text.
help.hint_2: Avoid choices unrelated to writing or checking file contents.

options.a: Use `with open(...)` so the file is handled cleanly.
options.b: Use `\n` when you want the next text on a new line.
options.c: Print the saved contents after writing so the result is visible.
options.d: Change the notes panel before writing.

dr-write-file-steps.title: Order the write-and-check steps
dr-write-file-steps.prompt: Put these steps in the correct order for a simple program that writes text and then verifies it.
dr-write-file-steps.hint: Write first, then reopen and display the saved text.

help.concept: A common workflow is open in write mode, write text, reopen in read mode, then print the contents.
help.hint_1: The file must be opened for writing before `write()` can happen.
help.hint_2: Printing comes after reading the saved contents back.

tokens.t3: Open the same path for reading

dr-append-log-sequence.title: Order the append workflow
dr-append-log-sequence.prompt: Arrange these actions to show how a program can create a starting log entry, append another entry, and then display the full log.
dr-append-log-sequence.hint: The first write creates the starting text before append adds more.

help.concept: To demonstrate append mode clearly, first create initial contents, then append new text, then read the whole file back.
help.hint_1: You need some starting text before the append step makes sense.
help.hint_2: Reading and printing the full log should happen after both writes are finished.

tokens.t1: Open the log with mode "w" and write the first line
tokens.t2: Open the log with mode "a" and add another line

ci-write-greeting-file.title: Write a personalized greeting file
ci-write-greeting-file.prompt: Read a name, write `Hello, NAME!` to `greeting.txt`, then open the file and print its contents.
ci-write-greeting-file.hint: Use `input()` for the name, then write the formatted greeting into the file.

help.concept: Writing text files lets a program save results that can be opened again later.
help.hint_1: Use a `with open(...) as file:` block for the write step.
help.hint_2: Open the same file again for reading when you need to print the saved contents.

ci-write-greeting-file.starterCode:
name = input()

# Write Hello, NAME! to greeting.txt.
# Then open greeting.txt and print its contents.

ci-overwrite-shopping-list.title: Overwrite a shopping list from input
ci-overwrite-shopping-list.prompt: Read two shopping items, overwrite `shopping.txt` with one item per line, then print the file contents.
ci-overwrite-shopping-list.hint: Opening a file with `"w"` replaces the old contents.

help.concept: Writing text files lets a program save results that can be opened again later.
help.hint_1: Use a `with open(...) as file:` block for the write step.
help.hint_2: Open the same file again for reading when you need to print the saved contents.

second_item = input()

# Write both items to shopping.txt, one per line.
# Then print the file contents.

ci-append-log-entry.prompt: Read one log entry, append it to `log.txt` on a new line, then print the full file contents.
ci-append-log-entry.hint: Opening a file with `"a"` keeps the old contents and adds new text at the end.

help.concept: Writing text files lets a program save results that can be opened again later.
help.hint_1: Use a `with open(...) as file:` block for the write step.
help.hint_2: Open the same file again for reading when you need to print the saved contents.

sketch-write-mode-basics.title: Write mode creates or replaces text
sketch-write-mode-basics.bodyMarkdown:
When you open a path with write mode (`"w"`), Python prepares that path for new text. If the path already has text, write mode replaces the old contents.

```python
with open("note.txt", "w") as file:
    file.write("Hello\n")

with open("note.txt") as file:
    print(file.read())
```

Step by step:

- `open("note.txt", "w")` opens the path for writing.
- `file.write("Hello\n")` puts text into the file.
- Opening the same path again in read mode lets you check what was saved.
- `print(file.read())` sends the saved text to the output panel.

In this example, the output is:

```python
Hello
```

Try it yourself: change `"Hello\n"` to a different message, click Run, and check the new output.

sketch-append-mode-basics.title: Append mode adds to the end
sketch-append-mode-basics.bodyMarkdown:
Append mode (`"a"`) keeps the existing text and adds new text at the end.

```python
with open("log.txt", "w") as file:
    file.write("Start\n")

with open("log.txt", "a") as file:
    file.write("Next\n")

with open("log.txt") as file:
    print(file.read())
```

Line by line:

- The first `with open(..., "w")` creates the starting text.
- The second `with open(..., "a")` adds another line without removing `Start`.
- Reading the file back shows both lines.

The output becomes:

```python
Start
Next
```

Your turn: change the second written line, run the code, and predict what the full file text will be before you check the output panel.

sketch-newlines-and-verifying.title: Use newline characters and verify what you wrote
sketch-newlines-and-verifying.bodyMarkdown:
When writing several lines, `\n` starts a new line. It is also helpful to reopen the file and print its contents so you can verify the result.

```python
with open("tasks.txt", "w") as file:
    file.write("Buy milk\nWash dishes\n")

with open("tasks.txt") as file:
    saved_text = file.read()
    print(saved_text)
```

What this does:

- `Buy milk\n` writes one line and moves to the next line.
- `Wash dishes\n` writes the second line.
- `file.read()` gets the full saved text.
- Printing `saved_text` lets you confirm the file contents in the output panel.

Without `\n`, both tasks would appear on the same line.

Try this: add a third task to the string, click Run, and check whether each task appears on its own line.
